ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Korean hot plate cheese corn. FOODON_00002809 (corn food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (maize/corn cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code for cheese-topped corn dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for hot plate cheese corn as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of hot plate cheese corn is approximately 200 g, served sizzling on a cast-iron plate with melted cheese over sweet corn.

nutrient_source.title: Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy 11168) combined with Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk (USDA SR Legacy 01009) and mayonnaise (USDA SR Legacy 04025); composite estimate for Korean corn cheese dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for hot plate cheese corn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients: sweet corn (~55%), mozzarella cheese (~25%), mayonnaise (~10%), butter (~5%), sugar and salt (~5%). Weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — sweet corn (boiled); Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean hot plate cheese corn. GI of 52 is based on sweet corn (boiled), GI = 52 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The addition of cheese, mayonnaise, and butter lowers the overall glycemic response due to fat and protein content; GI of 52 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 19.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving) / 100 = 20.6, rounded to 20.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~620 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as one serving provides approximately 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (52) and moderate glycemic load (~20.5 per serving) from sweet corn carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the sweet corn base contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 20.5 per standard serving; pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables can further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) from mozzarella cheese and mayonnaise
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should be aware that one serving provides approximately 9.6 g of saturated fat, representing roughly 48% of the recommended daily limit of 20 g; reduced-fat cheese or smaller portions are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Cheese Corn (石板芝士玉米; Korean: 핫플레이트 치즈 콘) is a popular Korean-origin side dish and snack consisting of sweet corn kernels topped with melted mozzarella cheese, mayonnaise, and butter, served sizzling on a cast-iron hot plate. The dish is characterised by its sweet, savoury, and creamy flavour profile, with a slightly caramelised corn base and a stretchy, golden cheese topping. It is widely served in Korean restaurants and Korean-influenced eateries across Singapore, including bibimbap and Korean rice bowl restaurants. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 396 kcal, 12.4 g protein, 23 g fat (including ~9.6 g saturated fat), and 39.6 g carbohydrate with a moderate glycemic load of ~20.5. The sodium content (~620 mg per serving) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian and typically halal, but contains dairy and egg allergens. It is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance. Clinically, the moderate GI of 52 (based on sweet corn) and the fat content from cheese and mayonnaise make portion control relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease risk.

How many calories in cheese corn?
Is cheese corn suitable for diabetics?
What allergens are in cheese corn?

What is 石板芝士玉米?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for hot plate cheese corn exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients using a weighted composite approach. GI is estimated from sweet corn (boiled) as the closest approved analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data for sweet corn (FDC ID 170108), mozzarella cheese whole milk (FDC ID 173420), and mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741), weighted by approximate ingredient proportions in the finished dish (corn 55%, mozzarella 25%, mayonnaise 10%, butter 5%, sugar and salt 5%). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the same USDA SR Legacy composite and are approximate. GI of 52 is based on sweet corn boiled (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). GL per serving = (52 × 19.8 × 200) / 10000 = 20.6, reported as 20.5. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record as the dish is cheese-dominant; however, a 'Mixed' classification could also be argued given the corn base. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields carry estimated numeric values from the USDA composite.
